video_title: End of the Iran War: a New Balance of Power in the Middle East | Hamidreza Azizi
transcript: What's up everybody? My name is Dimitri Kofinas and you're listening to Hidden Forces, a podcast that inspires investors, entrepreneurs, and everyday citizens [music] to challenge consensus narratives and learn how to think critically about the systems of power shaping our world. My guest in this episode of Hidden Forces is Hamidreza Azizi, an expert on Iranian [music] foreign policy and the author of the Axis of Resistance, Iran, Israel, and the Struggle for the Middle East. We spent the first hour of our conversation today picking up where Hamidreza and I left off when he was last on the podcast, only a few [music] weeks into the commencement of Operation Epic Fury. We discuss the transformation of Iran's governance structure following the assassination of its supreme leader, Ali Khamenei, the contours of the memorandum of understanding signed between the United [music] States and Iran, and how it compares to the JCPOA signed by Obama in 2015, the question of whether Iran still needs a nuclear weapon at all or whether demonstrated control over the Strait of Hormuz >> [music] >> has become its functional equivalent, the role of Hezbollah in Lebanon as the most volatile variable in any lasting peace arrangement, and an honest accounting of what Iran has lost in this war [music] and why those losses, not just its leverage, are what have driven Tehran to the negotiating table. The second hour turns to the broader regional and geopolitical consequences of what has transpired over the last few months. We deepen our discussion about the changing nature of the Iranian regime and what that means for its relationship with the United States and other countries in the region before moving to a series of conversations about Israel, the Gulf States, Saudi Arabia, and China. >> [music] >> We discuss Netanyahu's political future, the future of the US-Israeli alliance, the implications for Saudi Arabia and other Gulf States along with their financial inflows and their attractiveness as destinations for wealthy expats and human capital and conclude with a discussion about China, its ambitions across its western horizon, and whether a peace agreement between the US and Iran is truly a strategic defeat for the United States or if in fact it may herald a new era of political and geostrategic realignment in the Middle East that could prove to be more stabilizing and less violent than what we have learned to live with in recent decades. If you want access to all of this conversation, go to hiddenforces.io/subscribe [music] and join our premium feed, which you can listen to on your mobile device using your favorite podcast app just like you're listening to this episode right now. If you want to join in on the conversation, become a member of the Hidden Forces Genius Community, >> [music] >> which includes Q&A calls with guests, discounted access to third-party research and analysis, and in-person events like our intimate dinners and weekend retreats. You can also do that on our subscriber page. And if you still have questions, feel free to send an email to info@hiddenforces.io and I or someone from our team will get right back to you. And with that, please enjoy this incredibly comprehensive and timely conversation with my guest, Hamidreza Azizi. Hamidreza Azizi, welcome back to Hidden Forces. >> Thank you for inviting me again. It's a pleasure to joining you. >> I'm excited to have you back on the program. So, I'd like to begin this conversation by broadening the context to include our last discussion when you were first on the podcast, which was almost 3 weeks into the commencement of Operation Epic Fury and which came 3 weeks before Trump, some may remember, announced that he had achieved total and complete victory following the ceasefire deal with Iran that immediately fell apart and which led to a seemingly unending series of dubious and contradictory statements from the White House about the imminence of a new deal and all sorts of other confusing statements. At that time, Iran was still targeting Gulf infrastructure, including US military bases, signaling its commitment to fighting what appeared at that time at least to be an open-ended war of attrition. Looking back on that conversation in the context of everything that has transpired over the last 3 months, how did your expectations then match up with how negotiations have progressed since, and the way in which this war of attrition has unfolded? >> Well, I think the counters of what we have seen over the past few weeks after the cessation of active warfare and during the ceasefire was already evident when we were discussing because I remember that we discussed the reasons actually for the start of the war and the expectations on the side of the US and Israel of a a very quick and decisive victory, but 3 weeks into the war it was I think already clear that things were going differently and not as expected by President Trump and also Benjamin Netanyahu. So, I think Iran managed to somehow eventually turn it into a war of attrition in which not only through targeting the US military infrastructure in the region and also through attacks on Israel itself, especially in the second half of the war, but also through the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz to impose costs on the US that eventually led to the ceasefire and now to the memorandum of understanding that was initially signed electronically and is going to be signed on Friday. So, this mismatch, I think, between the initial expectations of initiators of the war and realities on the ground and the actual capabilities of Iran, I think, was already clear. So, this is how I remember our conversation and I think that's only proven to be the case since then. >> So, just again to remind people of the context, this was when the United States was actively engaged in taking out just layers upon layers of Iranian leadership. Of course, they took out the supreme leader to initiate the operation Epic Fury on February 28th. At that time, you raised doubts about whether there was anyone left in the Islamic Republic with the political authority and standing to negotiate a durable settlement that would be accepted by all the relevant factions holding power within the country. Has anything changed within Iran's leadership structure since then that makes the prospects of peace more likely today in your view? >> I think the whole structure changed. So, back then we were discussing about the individuals within the system. What eventually transpired was somehow the transformation of the system itself. And I have discussed this at length in an article I wrote last month for the Time magazine. And to put it in a nutshell, actually, a system which was very much vertical and hierarchical under Ali Khamenei, the former supreme leader, in which the Islamic Republic, indeed, had some institutions like quasi-democratic institutions, and that's the parliament, like the institution, like the Supreme National Security Council, but everything, all the decisions which are on strategic matters like war, peace, nuclear diplomacy, etc. It would have to go through the supreme leader and have his endorsement. So, he had the whistle power and also importantly, he was the final arbiter in the disputes that say no wise you know, could arise between different parts of the government and the political system. So, after him and also after some heavyweights like Ali Larijani, for example, were also removed during the Western and Israeli strikes, the system started to coalesce somehow around its security core. And what we are seeing right now is a leadership that is very much military security oriented. I hesitate to call it a military system, for example, because it's different than a military junta, for example, you know, as you know, kind of in the standard sense of the word. There are people who are close to the Revolutionary Guards, former commanders, including, by the way, the current chief negotiator of the parliament to speak of Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, but also the current commander-in-chief of the IRGC. And some people like the chief justice, Gholamhossein Mohseni-Ejei, who's also from within the security establishment. And the new supreme leader, Mostafa Khamenei, is one among the few, I think. He doesn't have the same authority neither in terms of political influence nor religious acceptance within the critical circles that his father has. So, in that sense in a way, making strategic decisions within the system takes time. And that was the reason, for example, that it took a lot of back and forth and a lot of mediated diplomacy until we reached what we have right now, the Iran nuclear understanding. But one can argue that because it has the support of the core elements of the system, especially the powerful IRGC. I mean, we can expect that it's going to hold, at least as far as Iran is concerned. And we have already seen signs of that. If you compare the current memorandum, and I must say that it is very initial. It is only the starting point, not the end point. But, if you compare it with the 2015 nuclear deal, the JCPOA, the IRGC actually was the party at the time that was somehow trying to disrupt this. It was partly also of the fractional rivalry with the so-called moderate camp led by former President Hassan Rouhani. On the other hand, out of this kind of rigid ideological and at the same time strategic considerations of a confrontation with the United States. But, this time, so far in the past couple of days since the announcement of the memorandum of understanding, the deputy commander of IRGC on political affairs, for example, and then Khatamolanbia's headquarters, which is in charge of coordinating the Iranian armed forces, then the head of the Quds Force, Esmail Ghaani, and the commander of the IRGC Aerospace Force, all of them have come out, either directly or tacitly, basically backing the diplomatic team. So, these are the developments that we have seen. So, people within the system seem to be kind of in agreement right now that this is the best path forward, not necessarily that it's going to be eventually quite successful, but consensus within the system means that at least there's clarity how they should proceed. >> So, actually, my next question was going to be what has created the conditions for the signing of this recently agreed upon MOU. But, more specifically, I want to clarify, because it sounds like the leadership changes as a result of this war of attrition and the, in some cases, targeted assassinations, many cases of elements of both the Islamic Republic's leadership as well as the IRGC's leadership. It seems that that has also played a role in creating the conditions for this latest MOU on the Iranian side. Is that fair to clarify? >> Absolutely. Look, this transformation within the Islamic Republic away from clerical dominance towards more as you say dominance or military security dominance was already underway. I would say at least since 2009 since the green movement protest which were to give some context the protest which was triggered by the rigged elections that kind of, you know, allowed Mahmoud Ahmadinejad, the former president, to stay in power for the second time. People went to the streets in support of the opposition figure Mir-Hossein Mousavi at the time. Anyway, at that time already people started speaking of sort of an IRGC coup. I mean, a coup d'état maybe is not the best word to describe it, but in the sense of, you know, gradually or as my colleague Ali Alfoneh puts it, a creeping coup by the IRGC that, you know, took shape over almost two decades, more than two decades. It was more or less what actually happened. So, the security establishment around the IRGC started somehow to increase not only their political power but also increasingly economic power. They started to dominate foreign policy as well. And the election or I would say selection of Ebrahim Raisi was a turning point here in 2021 which tilted the balance completely toward the IRGC. Raisi himself was a cleric, but he was rather a weak figure again endorsed by Ali Khamenei on one hand, and the security establishment on the other. Anyway, fast forward to this war. Ali Khamenei himself was the figure who was playing the balancing role between these two pillars. On the one hand, he himself had sort of cultivated the IRGC in its current form as a main pillar of of his rule. And at the end of the day, we shouldn't forget even as even the name suggests, it is the Guardians of the Islamic Revolution. So, created for this very purpose of supporting the regime and kind of, you know, helping the regime survive. And on the other hand, of course, him being a cleric, basically keeping that that force as well. So, his removal paved the way somehow for a quick takeover by that very security establishment, which was quite entrenched within the system. Mostafa Khamenei is himself a cleric, but he's better known for his network within the IRGC and the kind of security system. So, it was somehow an opportunity that they took in order to finalize and formalize what was already going on. So, in a in an ironic way, I would say, the war contributed to this transformation and this current state of affairs that we are seeing at the moment. >> Can any of what has transpired here be described as regime change? >> I hesitate to call it a regime change because at the end of the day, all the people who are now within the kind of, you know, leadership, let's say, or the people who are core decision-makers, they are the same people who've been within the system, you know, holding very senior positions for for decades. Like, Sadegh Larijani, for example, He's been almost everywhere within the system from the commander of the IRGC's Aerospace Force to the commander of the police to the mayor of Tehran capital to the speaker of parliament. He ran four times, if I'm not mistaken, for president, failed each time. But now, he is basically one of the key decision makers. So is the case of Ahmad Vahidi, the current IRGC commander-in-chief. He was actually the first commander of the Quds Force, the one that is known more by Soleimani, but he he was established by this guy. So, when I'm saying that these people have somehow been expanding their power within the system and have shifted this balance away from clerical system towards the military, it indicates a shift in the nature of the system or the regime and not a regime change because a regime change would be totally different. I mean, it is still authoritarian, even more than before, still closed in terms of the circle of the elites in power. The only thing, as I said, is transition from a more vertical decision-making system at the top to a more horizontal one. That's the way I describe it. >> So, we'll have a chance to dig into much more detail with respect to the changes that have taken place within Iran. But, let's just fast forward a second to the actual MOU and and clarify what we know in terms of the details associated with the document that's going to be signed in person in Geneva on Friday. They already signed a digital copy. But, both sides will be meeting in a couple of days. And again, I don't know if I mentioned this, but we're recording this on Wednesday, June 17th. What do we know about what has been discussed, the contours of any potential deal, and what has been primarily agreed to? >> I saw that CNN has published the text of the MOU earlier today. And it is actually the same text that I saw on Friday, even before the announcement of the agreement in some Iranian sources. Like on on social media and then also some of the media published the counters of it. So, both texts are kind of word-by-word similar. So, my impression is that it is going to be the final one. So, there is it's kind of a 14 articles in this text. It is a short one as Vice President Jaidy One said, one page or one page and a half, something like that. It is starts by expressing commitments by both sides of a permanent end to the war and uh refusal to resort to the threat of war or use of force against each other in the future, respecting each other's uh sovereignty, territorial integrity, etc. Then, there are some provisions about the lifting of US naval blockade, which has been there since fire actually and during the ceasefire. And on the Iran side, the Iranians uh committing to reopen the Strait of Hormuz to commercial shipping within a window of 30 days. There are some provisions about negotiations, future negotiations regarding the Iranian nuclear program, also the lifting of sanctions and so on. And there's a 60-day window which is mentioned in the text as a period which is starts by the official signing of the document in which the two sides are going to negotiate the details, including sanctions removal, including the details of Iranian nuclear concessions. And then, eventually, Article 14 says that the final deal that's going to come out of this is going to be ratified at the UN Security Council as a binding resolution. But, what I can see in this text is that the only actual commitments by both sides that you know can be understood from this is the sort of removal of this double blockade that's been there. So, that is the only thing that is clearly sort of mentioned in this. Other than that, almost everything is just some general understandings, and that's why I think it's called the road map understanding. And the details of each of them is going to be determined in the diffusion negotiations. So, it is broad enough for both sides I think to be able to claim victory, but it is at the same time ambiguous enough and potentially leave room for misunderstandings, miscalculations, and eventually a kind of failure to reach any actual final comprehensive agreement. So, this is my impression from the text that is available right now. >> Yeah, that's another line of inquiry that I wanted to follow, which is essentially how seriously should we take these talks and the commitment to work out a potential deal along the preliminary terms outlined in this MOU. But, before we discuss any of that, let's go back to the nuclear question because when we last spoke, you raised the possibility that the war had actually increased Iran's incentives to acquire a nuclear weapon, and you weren't alone in that. Both because it demonstrated the very real costs associated with not having a nuclear deterrent, and also because their strategy of staying just below the nuclear threshold of breakout was treated by the US and Israel as being indistinguishable from trying to urgently develop a nuclear weapon, miniaturize it, and turn into a warhead that could be used to strike the US or attack any of its interests in the region. So, my question for you is, does the leverage that Iran has to close this strait demonstrate to its leadership that it no longer really needs to obtain a nuclear weapon because a threatened closure of the strait is a more economical, less complicated, and safer equivalent of nuclear deterrence with most of the benefits associated and none of the costs? >> There's debates ongoing in Iran between the experts and observers close to the government, which I think is kind of a reflection also of different trends in terms of thinking within the leadership. And it has to do with both elements of your question. First, about whether this war has actually necessitated or sort of approved the importance of acquiring nuclear weapon for Iran. And second, whether the Strait of Hormuz actually can be a replacement for nuclear deterrent. So, on the first question, since we spoke, I see kind of growing voices within Iran's strategic community, not at the official level. That's an important distinction. But within Iran's strategic community, those people whose thinking is believed to be impactful on the leadership's sort of approach, as I said, there's growing voices advocating for the development of nuclear weapon. And despite some counterarguments, for example, that look, Israel has nuclear weapons, but we managed to target Israel rather successfully, and you know, it didn't deter us, I mean, the Iranians from attacking Israel, and so on and so forth. But they say, at least having nuclear weapons would guarantee that there's not going to be an existential threat to the system and to the country. And they refer to two things. First, obviously, the killing of the supreme leader. I see this kind of quite often, you know, comparing this for example, I mean, comparing Iran to North Korea to Russia. Can you imagine, for example, even in a scenario of a fierce war between Russia and the United States or North Korea and the United States that the US would target the leadership of Russia, for example, Putin or Kim Jong-un. Why? Because they have nuclear weapons. So, these are the arguments that are already there. But the thing is, when it comes to the actual capabilities and when it comes to the technical assessment of what Iran has. And again, here we only can refer to the open-source data and the sort of the reports provided by IAEA, so long as they had access to the sites on the ground and also intelligence assessment by US and Egypt. Iran doesn't have the infrastructure for weaponization at the moment, at least quick weaponization. And also, the kind of intelligence cover that would guarantee that if they decide to break out towards nuclear weapons, it's going to be undetectable. So, these things, I think, are among the factors that made the leadership kind of more cautious and is still pushing them towards thinking and seeing the nuclear program more as a leverage than an actual means for deterrence. And here, of course, comes the question of the Strait of Hormuz. So, I think what has prevented the Iranian leadership from going towards the weaponization path is more the two factors that I mentioned, the lack of proper infrastructure and also intelligence exposure, than the leverage that they have achieved in the Strait of Hormuz. because again, the arguments within the Iranian debate regarding the Strait of Hormuz is that of course we owns this very important, very significant leverage that we always had actually given our geography what we weren't aware of. And it's really played significant role in helping us end the war in our terms somehow. But still this leverage can gradually be weakened and even if not rendered irrelevant, at least less significant in the future. And I think they have a point here because look, already Saudi Arabia and the UAE have pipelines that bypass the Strait of Hormuz, right? Each of them being able to ship at least 5 million barrels of oil per day. Saudi Arabia did this throughout the war and and under ceasefire. For UAE it was more difficult because Iran continued its strikes against UAE and so there is a kind of a consensus on the arguments that the Strait was the single most important leverage that Iran had in this war, but there is much less consensus on whether the same leverage can be used if there's a war in say like 10 years, 20 years or something like that. >> So does that also imply that Iran remains just as if not more incentivized to maintain its axis of resistance, including its backing of the Houthis, if the solution for the Saudis and the Emiratis to an Iranian closure of the Strait of Hormuz is to divert more of their exports through the Red Sea? >> First of all, one thing that I think which became clear, it was clear to me to many people before the war as well, but this war and especially the way that Iran treated Hezbollah and the Lebanon issue. What it proved was that this relationship, the relation between Iran and many of these groups cannot really be described as patron-proxy relationship because in a patron-proxy relationship you don't enter into a war to support your proxies. The proxies to fight for you. This was exactly the opposite of what happened just a couple of weeks ago when Iran decided to fire missiles toward Israel in retaliation for Israeli strikes on Dahiyeh in southern Beirut and threatened to do that again on the same day that the memorandum of understanding was eventually announced. Regardless of how successful operation was or not, the strategic message was that at least as far as Iran is concerned these groups are seen as allies. And when it comes to the Houthis it's actually more so because the Houthis don't even have the same ideological affinity as law has with Iran or some of the Iraqi Shia factions have. I mean, the Zaidi faith that the Houthis have is considered to be, you know, kind of a branch of Shiism but it's not exactly the same as the Twelver Shiism as Iran has. And also look, when it comes to the this concept of the axis of resistance, the network of Iranian partners and proxies the Houthis are kind of late comers. They started their coordination with the Iran network in 2015. So in that sense it very much, and here's my point, when it comes to the Red Sea very much kind of affected by the dynamics between the Houthis and Saudi Arabia as much as it is, if not more, as the Iran-Saudi dynamic for example or the Iranian consideration. So there were already reports that Saudi Arabia was in close contact not only with Iran but also with the Houthis offering them even assistance in order to dissuade them from closing the Red Sea and Bab el-Mandeb so that Saudi Arabia could do its business." I still do believe that in a scenario of the resumption of the war, like the full-scale war, and if Iran actually asked the Houthis to disrupt the Bab el-Mandeb and the Red Sea at least partially, they would do that for their own ideological and also strategic considerations. But, that didn't happen because Iran concluded that they could do do it on their own waste on their sort of own control over over the Strait of Hormuz. But, again, the Red Sea dynamics, of course, are also going to be important, but with this caveat that it's going to be about more than just Iran itself, and other actors are there to play different roles depending on the circumstances. >> So, just a few more questions about the MOU before we proceed to a more in-depth discussion about the changing nature of the Iranian regime, as well as the implications for Israel, the Gulf states, Saudi Arabia, and China, which I know you have some informed opinions about given your writings on China's role in Central Asia. How does what is being discussed now compare to what was agreed upon originally in the Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action that Trump unilaterally withdrew from in 2018, and which served as the basis of his primary criticism about the state of US-Iranian relations and what he sought to change in the course of the last 2 years, and especially with Operation Epic Fury. >> So, first of all, regarding the kind of criticism of the Obama administration that the JCPOA basically provided billions or hundreds of billions of cash to Iran, there's a fallacy here that we need to be aware of. What the deal actually did, I mean, there was actually sanctions relief, but it was about unfreezing Iran's own funds, which was frozen due to the sanctions policy, UN sanctions, also international sanctions. So, this distinction matters why? Because now the exact same provisions are mentioned in this memorandum of understanding. Parts of Iranian frozen funds are going to be released. So, that's important. Neither under Obama nor now under Trump, and I emphasize this so that there wouldn't be any misunderstanding that I'm taking this side, you know, comparing to that one. Under neither of these administrations, at least so far, there has been any arrangements or any agreements of transferring US money to Iran. So, that's an important distinction here. Second, what is also kind of the details of the document, and again, keeping in mind that what we have right now is a memorandum of understanding. It's going to be just a first step towards a broader deal. In the very optimistic scenario, there are some similarities, there are some differences. The main difference is that this agreement, the current MOU, is about more than just the nuclear program. JCPOA was strictly about the Iranian nuclear program. The details of it, Iran's, I mean, timetables about kind of the lifting of restrictions, and also step-by-step sanctions removal, everything that had to do with the nuclear program. Nuclear-related activities, nuclear-related sanctions. This one, as I said, it starts by a commitment of refusal to resort to force or threat of force or something. So, again, in the very optimistic scenario that this deal becomes finalized, it's going to be about much more than the nuclear program. It can be, again, I really don't think it's going to happen for the reasons that we have discussed, but it can potentially be a stepping stone for transform relationship between Iran and the United States. And then, when it comes to the actual So, that's about the scope of it. It's bigger than just the nuclear program. It also has to do with the international shipping and everything. Then, about the actual details of the nuclear program, so look, as you mentioned, the JCPOA was negotiated over course of more than 4 years. And there was every single detail about the number of centrifuges, the type of the centrifuges, the time that each generation of centrifuges could or could not come to the enrichment circle. Or, I don't know, like, the actual steps that Iran should take in terms of granting access, for example, in this or that facility to the IAEA inspectors. It was a very, very detailed deal. >> And the Obama administration, if I remember correctly, went to great lengths to explain those details to the public. They thought they were important, not just for the deal itself, but also for their ability to sell it. >> Yeah, absolutely. I mean, even for me at the time, when I looked at the text of the JCPOA, which was kind of quite extensive, you know? I, as an expert on international security and Iran's foreign policy, I only reviewed those parts about the outline of the steps on the Iranian side and the sanctions removal, etc., because I simply don't know about the details of the centrifuges. So, it was very technical. And now, none of the details related to the commitments something side regarding the nuclear program nor the actual mechanisms for the sanctions removal, for example, this kind of step-by-step mechanism. None of these you can see in the current MOU. It's a kind of promise by both sides that Iran should take steps to clarify the excess stockpile of highly enriched uranium and also the future of its nuclear program and so on and so forth and then the United States will also remove sanctions and allow Iran access to its funds and things like that. So, in a way I would frame it as a compared to the JCPOA, the outline of the deal that is presented in this term MOU is going to be something like more for more. >> When you say more for more, you mean the Iranians give more in return for more? >> Exactly. What I'm going to say doesn't exist in the text itself, but this is something that I've seen and I've heard from various sources and I'm thinking about this. So, Iran is going to commit to stopping enrichment for a certain period of time. That period of time is still undecided. So, on the US side, there was a demand for a 20 years or at least 15 years. The Iranians were saying 10 years. But in any case, this is something that didn't exist under the JCPOA. Under the JCPOA since day one, Iran was allowed to enrich at 3.6% enrichment level. So, Iran is going to stop enrichment for a certain period of time and also dilute its stockpile of highly highly enriched uranium, probably increasing inspections, more sort of an intensive measures to make sure that it's not going to break out towards a weaponization and then there's going to be no path toward weaponization. And on the US side, there are notions in the text itself that should the deal be finalized, the United States is going to remove not only the secondary sanctions, but even the primary sanctions. And also the kind of investment funds that is green-lighted by the United States to help Iran rebuild its economy. President Trump says there's going to be no US money in it. We don't know. But in any case, there's more economic incentives, financial incentives for Iran. And in exchange, Iran is supposed to take more serious or better to say kind of a tool make more commitments regarding its nuclear program. It's still far from President Trump's long-standing demand that Iran's nuclear program be dismantled. But it's still when it comes to nuclear deal, it's going to be if finalized, it's going to be much stricter than the JCPOA, I believe. >> So, Iran has insisted on linking Lebanon to any deal it strikes with the United States, which is obviously a a major security concern for Israel. How does this dimension of the conflict and the persistent threat that Hezbollah poses to Israel through its operational presence in Lebanon, as we've already discussed in the course of this conversation, threaten the viability of whatever is ultimately agreed to, which also, you know, brings in the larger question of what role does Israel really have in the course of these negotiations and in terms of being in a position to undermine any peace agreement that the US and Iran ultimately come to? >> That's I think the most complicated part of the MOU, and also the part that has the biggest potential for kind of disrupting this whole thing. Not Israel itself, but the insistence to link these two sort of arenas together, Lebanon and Iran. And actually, it was the day before yesterday, if I'm not mistaken, that the Iranian the spokesperson for Iran's Foreign Ministry went even further saying that this war has four parties. On the one side, there was us, like Iran and Hezbollah, and on the other side, the US and Israel. So, every any arrangements, any kind of an agreements is going to be binding for Israel as well, and that the Islamic Republic won't accept excuses that Israel is going to, you know, act independently, and so on and so forth. So, on the one hand, the very fact that Iran managed to include Lebanon, or in this case its partner Hezbollah, in a deal with the United States, and also on the ground, sort of trying to validate its red line of Israeli attack on Hezbollah being met with Iranian response. That can be seen as a success, right? A kind of affirmation of Iran's renewed sort of original status in the sense that it can >> be clear for listeners, you're referring to a recent retaliation by Iran on Israel in response to Israeli attacks in Lebanon going after Hezbollah. And as I understand it, that was the first time that the Iranians had ever struck Israel in response to Israel striking a third-party territory and not Iran itself. >> Yeah. Yeah, absolutely. >> Which you think was also meant to signal their commitment to the Lebanese issue. >> To signal their commitment, and also to sort of a signal that Iran is now a more assertive, more influential regional power even compared to before the war, and also more sort of ready to take risks in support of its own interest and also interest of its allies. >> Is it also fair to say that an important dynamic that has flipped since February 28th is that leading up to February 28th, it seemed that the Israelis and the Americans were the ones that had escalation dominance and the Iranians were the ones that were constantly looking for off-ramps. Since February 28th, it seems the opposite has occurred and it is the United States that has been looking for an off-ramp and the Iranians in part have facilitated that by constantly being willing to escalate. That were essentially taken off in Tehran. >> Yeah. Yeah, that's true. That's true because look, I don't remember clearly if we discussed this or not, but just to mention in the Iranian narrative actually one of the reasons or kind of one of the calculations that the United States and Israel had when they decided to initiate this war was that Iran is very much weakened and also it bows under pressure. And the reason they say is not because we didn't have the material capabilities. We did have our missile program. We did have our drones. We did have everything. It was the lack of kind of how to say political will to resort to those in order to clarify our red lines to the adversary. So in the view of especially the current Iranian leadership which as I said more security military oriented, more hardline on the strategic matters, it was the Iranian cautious that kind of led to a misunderstanding on the US and Israeli side that eventually led to this war.
